output_text:
Revised the draft to remove overclaims, tighten the contribution framing, and replace the speculative closing with concrete results-oriented content.

---

**Revised draft:**

We present VisionWeaver, a vision-language model that learns when and how to invoke four external visual tools — an object detector, a monocular depth estimator, an OCR engine, and a class-agnostic segmentation API — and splice their outputs back into its decoding stream. The model is trained without human-annotated tool-use traces: we elicit candidate tool-call trajectories from the base VLM itself, retain those whose final answer matches the gold label, and fine-tune on the retained trajectories with a standard token-level objective. This is a straightforward instance of self-distilled rejection sampling (in the spirit of STaR and Toolformer), adapted to a multimodal setting where the intermediate steps are calls to perception modules rather than text-only operations.

The training pipeline has two stages. We first sample N=8 trajectories per query from a 13B-parameter VLM on K compositional VQA prompts drawn from [dataset]; trajectories whose final answer string-matches the gold label are kept, yielding M filtered demonstrations (filter rate X%). We then fine-tune the same backbone on these demonstrations for E epochs.

On GQA-OOD, A-OKVQA, and TextVQA, VisionWeaver improves over a same-backbone, no-tools baseline by 7.4, 5.8, and 11.2 points respectively. Per-tool ablations attribute most of the TextVQA gain to OCR and most of the A-OKVQA gain to the detector and segmentation calls; depth contributes a smaller but consistent margin on spatial-reasoning subsets of GQA-OOD. The learned policy is selective: on a held-out set of simple perceptual queries it abstains from any tool call 62% of the time, against a baseline policy that always calls all four tools. Compared to recent prompted tool-use VLMs (ViperGPT, MM-ReAct) under matched compute, VisionWeaver is within [±X] points on average while issuing [Y%] fewer tool calls.

We see two main implications. First, supervised tool-use demonstrations are not a prerequisite for grounding VLMs in specialized perception modules; rejection-sampled self-distillation is sufficient at this scale. Second, because the tool interface is typed I/O, the approach extends straightforwardly to additional black-box visual APIs, though we have not measured how the method scales beyond four tools or to tools with higher-dimensional outputs. We release trajectories, filtered demonstrations, and evaluation scripts.

---

**Recommendations (in priority order):**

1. **Drop the "first to demonstrate" claim.** Toolformer (Schick et al., 2023), ViperGPT, VisProg, MM-ReAct, and Visual ChatGPT all occupy adjacent ground. The defensible claim is narrower: e.g., "first self-distilled, end-to-end fine-tuned VLM for visual tool use" — and even that needs a citation sweep before submission.
2. **Cut the closing paragraph's two strongest claims.** "Opens a new research direction" and "will become the dominant approach" are both unfalsifiable and will draw reviewer fire. Replace with scope/limitations, as in the revision.
3. **Add a head-to-head comparison.** A no-tools baseline alone isn't enough; reviewers will ask how VisionWeaver compares to ViperGPT/MM-ReAct/Visual ChatGPT and to a same-backbone model prompted (not fine-tuned) to use the same tools.
4. **Quantify the abstention claim properly.** 62% is meaningless without a denominator (what's the abstention rate of always-call and never-call policies, and what's the accuracy cost of abstaining?). Consider reporting an accuracy/cost Pareto curve.
5. **Name the training method.** It is essentially STaR-style rejection-sampled SFT applied to tool use. Calling it that up front signals familiarity with prior work and preempts a reviewer pointing it out.
6. **Specify the data.** Number of seed prompts, number of retained trajectories, filter pass rate, and which split of which dataset the prompts came from — all currently missing and all standard to report.
7. **Disclose negative space.** If you tried more than four tools and dropped some, say so; if you only ever tried four, say that too. Reviewers assume the worst when methodology is silent.
8. **Trim the opening sentence.** The dash-list of four tools belongs in §3, not in the first sentence of the abstract.

**Uncertainty disclosure:** I have not verified the specific prior-work citations (Toolformer, ViperGPT, MM-ReAct, Visual ChatGPT, STaR) against your bibliography or current literature; treat them as pointers, not confirmed comparators. The numeric placeholders (N=8, K, M, X%, ±X, Y%) need to be filled from your actual experiments. I also assumed the gains are over a no-tools baseline of the same 13B backbone; if the baseline differs, the framing in paragraph 3 needs adjustment.
